skill.name: coding-standards
skill.description: Coding standards and best practices for TypeScript, JavaScript, React, Node.js, API design, testing, and maintainable project structure.
skill.summary: Apply practical coding standards for TypeScript, React, APIs, testing, and maintainable JavaScript projects.
skill.author: affaan-m

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, dynamic import, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found these are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, instructional examples, and ordinary words such as description and Design. No executable payloads, prompt injection attempts, credential access, or data exfiltration behavior were found in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The external command detections correspond to Markdown fenced code blocks and example snippets. They are documentation examples, not Ruby backtick execution or commands run by the skill.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Every listed location is inside SKILL.md documentation examples or code fence delimiters. The skill contains no executable script files or runtime command invocation path.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Fetch API Appears Only in Error Handling Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The network detections are sample TypeScript functions demonstrating error handling. They do not define a skill runtime, fixed external endpoint, secret transfer, or data exfiltration flow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fetch calls are contained in a section named Error Handling and use a generic url parameter. No hardcoded host, credential source, or outbound collection behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Dynamic Import Used as React Lazy Loading Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The dynamic import detection is a React lazy loading example for a local component. It does not load remote code or execute untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The import target is a local relative module in a performance best practices snippet. There is no variable-controlled import path or runtime loader supplied by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Security Keywords Matched in Prose and API Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Weak cryptography and system reconnaissance detections are keyword matches in normal documentation text, API route examples, comments, and naming guidance. No cryptographic algorithm use or host reconnaissance command appears.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain front matter, words such as description or Design, REST route examples, and code quality prose. I found no evidence of MD5, DES usage, uname, whoami, or similar reconnaissance execution.

content.value_statement: Teams lose time when code style, structure, and review expectations are unclear. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical standards for cleaner TypeScript, React, Node.js, APIs, and tests.
actual_capabilities: Guides naming, readability, simplicity, immutability, and type safety decisions.
Provides TypeScript and JavaScript examples for functions, data models, async code, and validation.
Defines React patterns for typed components, hooks, state updates, and conditional rendering.
Covers API response formats, REST conventions, input validation, and project organization.
Adds review guidance for comments, JSDoc, performance, tests, and common code smells.
limitations: It is a guidance-only Markdown skill and does not run linters or formatters.
Examples are general and may need adjustment for a project-specific framework or style guide.
It does not include security hardening standards beyond validation and error handling examples.
It cannot enforce standards automatically without separate tooling in the repository.

use_cases.title: Standardize Pull Request Reviews
use_cases.description: Use the skill to ask Claude or Codex to review pull requests against consistent TypeScript, React, API, and testing expectations.

use_cases.title: Improve New Feature Code
use_cases.description: Apply the standards while building components, routes, hooks, and utility functions so new code is easier to read and maintain.

use_cases.title: Onboard Contributors
use_cases.description: Give contributors a shared reference for naming, file structure, validation, tests, and documentation before they submit changes.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Small Change
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a focused diff
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this change using the coding-standards skill. Focus on naming, readability, immutability, and type safety. List the most important fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this React component using the coding-standards skill. Improve props typing, state updates, conditional rendering, and naming while preserving behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Design an API Route
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the coding-standards skill to design this API route. Include request validation, response shape, error handling, and test cases in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Module for Maintainability
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this module with the coding-standards skill. Identify code smells, duplication, deep nesting, weak tests, unclear boundaries, and prioritized remediation steps.

output_examples.input: Review a TypeScript utility module for maintainability.
output: Findings are grouped by naming, type safety, error handling, and duplication.
Each issue includes a concise reason and a recommended change.
The review separates required fixes from optional cleanup.

output_examples.input: Improve a React form component before review.
output: The response recommends clearer prop names, typed event handlers, and safer state updates.
It flags deeply nested rendering and suggests smaller helper components.
It preserves the existing behavior while improving readability.

output_examples.input: Plan a new API endpoint for creating records.
output: The answer outlines validation, success responses, error responses, and pagination or metadata needs.
It calls out tests for valid input, invalid input, and failed dependencies.

best_practices: Provide the relevant file or diff so the assistant can apply standards to real code.
Ask for prioritized findings when using the skill for pull request review.
Combine this skill with project-specific lint, format, and test commands for enforcement.
anti_patterns: Do not treat the examples as a complete security policy or compliance checklist.
Do not apply every recommendation blindly when project conventions intentionally differ.
Do not use the skill as a replacement for automated tests, type checks, or linters.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or install tools?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guidance skill that provides standards and examples for AI-assisted coding work.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover best?
faq.answer: It focuses on TypeScript, JavaScript, React, Node.js, REST APIs, and common frontend project structure.

faq.question: Can it enforce formatting automatically?
faq.answer: No. Use separate formatter, linter, type checker, and test tools to enforce rules in a repository.

faq.question: Is it useful for code reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a consistent checklist for readability, types, tests, and maintainability.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It includes basic validation and error handling patterns, but it is not a full secure coding standard.

faq.question: Can teams adapt the recommendations?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams should combine the guidance with their own architecture, naming, testing, and framework conventions.
